q: What were the ancient beliefs regarding blood distribution?
a: Blood was made in the liver and distributed via veins, while air from the lungs was distributed via arteries.

q: Who was William Harvey and what did he demonstrate?
a: A 17th-century English physician who demonstrated that the heart pumps blood in a closed, recirculating loop (a one-way circuit guided by valves), refuting the idea that tissues consume all delivered blood.

q: Who was Marcello Malpighi and what was his contribution?
a: An Italian anatomist who discovered capillaries in the 17th century, providing the missing structural link in Harvey's closed-loop theory.

q: What three elements comprise the cardiovascular system?
a: A series of tubes (blood vessels), fluid (blood), and a pump (heart).

q: From where does oxygen enter the body, and what is its transport destination?
a: Enters from lungs; transported to all cells.

q: From where do nutrients and water enter the body, and what is their transport destination?
a: Enter from intestinal tract; transported to all cells.

q: What is the origin and transport destination of wastes moved from cell to cell?
a: Originates from some cells; transported to the liver for processing.

q: Where are immune cells, antibodies, and clotting proteins present, and what is their transport destination?
a: Present in blood continuously; available to any cell that needs them.

q: What is the origin and transport destination of hormones moved from cell to cell?
a: Originates from endocrine cells; transported to target cells.

q: What is the origin and transport destination of stored nutrients moved from cell to cell?
a: Originates from the liver and adipose tissue; transported to all cells.

q: What is the origin and transport destination of metabolic wastes leaving the body?
a: Originates from all cells; transported to kidneys.

q: What is the origin and transport destination of heat leaving the body?
a: Originates from all cells; transported to skin.

q: What is the origin and transport destination of carbon dioxide leaving the body?
a: Originates from all cells; transported to lungs.

q: Define Hypoxia.
a: A state of low oxygen.

q: How quickly do neurons lose consciousness and suffer permanent damage during hypoxia?
a: Loss of consciousness within 5-10 seconds of flow stoppage; permanent brain damage within 5-10 minutes.

q: What does homeostatic control prioritize above all else during hypoxia?
a: Cerebral blood flow.

q: What makes up the functional network of the Cardiovascular System?
a: The heart, blood vessels (vasculature), and the cells and plasma of the blood.

q: Define Arteries.
a: Blood vessels that carry blood away from the heart.

q: Define Veins.
a: Blood vessels that return blood to the heart.

q: What is the structural definition and function of the Septum?
a: The central dividing wall of the heart that separates it into left and right, independently functioning halves.

q: Define Atrium.
a: The upper chamber of the heart that receives blood returning from the blood vessels.

q: Define Ventricle.
a: The lower chamber of the heart that pumps blood out into the blood vessels.

q: Define Cyanosis.
a: A condition causing a bluish coloration of the skin (around the mouth and under fingernails) resulting from lower oxygen content in the blood.

q: What is the Pulmonary Circulation pathway?
a: The pathway of blood from the right ventricle to the lungs and back to the left atrium.

q: What is Step 1 of the Pulmonary Circuit process?
a: Deoxygenated blood flows from the right atrium into the right ventricle.

q: What is Step 2 of the Pulmonary Circuit process?
a: Blood is pumped through the pulmonary trunk/arteries to the lungs.

q: What is Step 3 of the Pulmonary Circuit process?
a: Blood is oxygenated at the exchange surfaces of the lungs.

q: What is Step 4 of the Pulmonary Circuit process?
a: Oxygenated blood returns through pulmonary veins to the left atrium.

q: What is the Systemic Circulation pathway?
a: The pathway of blood from the left side of the heart to the tissues and back to the right side of the heart.

q: What is Step 1 of the Systemic Circuit process?
a: Oxygenated blood flows from the left atrium to the left ventricle.

q: What is Step 2 of the Systemic Circuit process?
a: Blood is pumped from the left ventricle into the aorta.

q: What is Step 3 of the Systemic Circuit process?
a: The aorta branches into ascending arteries (to arms, head, brain) and descending/abdominal arteries (to trunk, liver, digestive tract, kidneys, pelvis, legs).

q: What is Step 4 of the Systemic Circuit process?
a: Arteries divide into capillary networks where exchange occurs (oxygen diffuses into tissues).

q: What is Step 5 of the Systemic Circuit process?
a: Deoxygenated blood enters the venous side, moving to small veins, then to larger ascending/descending veins.

q: What is Step 6 of the Systemic Circuit process?
a: Veins converge into the superior vena cava (upper body) and inferior vena cava (lower body).

q: What is Step 7 of the Systemic Circuit process?
a: Venae cavae empty into the right atrium.

q: What is Coronary Circulation?
a: The first branch of the aorta contains the coronary arteries, which nourish the heart muscle itself and empty into the right atrium at the coronary sinus.

q: What are Portal Systems?
a: A special arrangement where two capillary beds are joined in series before returning to the heart.

q: What is the Hepatic Portal System and its function?
a: Connects the digestive tract capillaries directly to the liver capillaries (hepatic portal vein). Allows the liver to process nutrients and detoxify foreign substances before general circulation.

q: What is the Renal Portal System?
a: Two capillary beds connected in series in the kidneys.

q: What is the Hypothalamic-hypophyseal Portal System?
a: Connects the hypothalamus and the anterior pituitary.

q: What is the flow mechanism for liquids and gases?
a: They flow down pressure gradients (Delta P) from regions of higher pressure to regions of lower pressure.

q: Define Hydrostatic Pressure.
a: The pressure exerted by a fluid that is not moving on the walls of its container. Proportional to the height of the fluid column. Exerted equally in all directions.

q: Define Hydraulic Pressure.
a: The pressure of moving fluids. Contains a dynamic, flowing component (kinetic energy) and a lateral component (hydrostatic/potential energy).

q: What is the normal Systemic Blood Pressure Range?
a: High of 93 mm Hg in the aorta down to a few mm Hg in the venae cavae.

q: Define Driving Pressure.
a: The high pressure created in the ventricles by contracting muscle that is transferred to the blood, driving it through the blood vessels.

q: What happens to the pressure inside a fluid-filled container if its walls contract?
a: Pressure inside increases.

q: What happens to the pressure inside a fluid-filled container if its walls expand?
a: Pressure inside decreases.

q: How does vasoconstriction affect blood pressure?
a: Decreased vessel diameter increases blood pressure.

q: How does vasodilation affect blood pressure?
a: Increased vessel diameter decreases blood pressure.

q: What is the Pressure Gradient (Delta P) and what does it determine?
a: The difference in pressure between two ends of a tube (P1 - P2). It determines flow, not absolute pressure.

q: What is the mathematical relationship between Flow and the Pressure Gradient?
a: Flow is directly proportional to the pressure gradient.

q: Define Resistance (R) in the cardiovascular system.
a: The tendency of the cardiovascular system to oppose blood flow, caused by friction between the fluid and vessel walls, and friction among cells within the blood.

q: What is the mathematical relationship between Flow and Resistance?
a: Flow is inversely proportional to resistance (Flow = 1 / R).

q: What does Poiseuilles Law explain?
a: The relationship of factors influencing resistance for fluid flowing through a tube.

q: What is the equation for Poiseuilles Law?
a: R = 8 L eta / pi r^4.

q: In Poiseuilles Law, what does L represent?
a: Length of the tube (essentially constant in human body).

q: In Poiseuilles Law, what does eta represent?
a: Viscosity of the fluid (normally constant, determined by ratio of red blood cells to plasma).

q: In Poiseuilles Law, what does r represent?
a: Radius of the tube (the primary variable determining resistance).

q: How does changing the radius affect resistance and flow according to Poiseuilles Law?
a: A small change in radius causes a massive change in resistance (R is proportional to 1/r^4). Doubling the radius decreases resistance to 1/16th and increases flow 16-fold.

q: What is the Combined Flow Equation?
a: Blood flow is directly proportional to the pressure gradient and inversely proportional to the resistance (Flow = Delta P / R).

q: Define Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP).
a: The primary driving force for blood flow.

q: What is the equation for Mean Arterial Pressure (MAP)?
a: Mean arterial pressure is proportional to cardiac output x peripheral resistance.

q: Define Flow Rate (Q).
a: The volume of blood that passes a given point in the system per unit time. Expressed in L/min or mL/min. Measures how much.

q: Define Velocity of Flow (v).
a: The distance a fixed volume of blood travels in a given period of time. Expressed in cm/min or mm/sec. Measures how fast.

q: What is the equation relating Velocity of Flow to Flow Rate?
a: Velocity equals flow rate divided by cross-sectional area (v = Q / A).

q: If flow rate is constant, how does velocity vary with diameter?
a: Velocity varies inversely with diameter (fluid flows faster through narrow sections and slower through wide sections).

q: What is the anatomical location of the heart?
a: In the center of the thoracic cavity on the ventral side, sandwiched between the lungs.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Pericardium?
a: Encasing the external surface of the heart.

q: What is the physiological function of the Pericardium?
a: Protects the heart; contains a thin layer of pericardial fluid to lubricate the external surface as the heart beats.

q: Define Pericarditis.
a: Inflammation of the pericardium causing a friction rub.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Myocardium?
a: Forms the bulk of the heart walls, covered by inner and outer epithelium/connective tissue.

q: What is the physiological function of the Myocardium?
a: Contracts to pump blood.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Apex of the heart?
a: Angles down to the left side of the body, resting on the diaphragm.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Base of the heart?
a: Lies just behind the sternum (breastbone).

q: What is the physiological function of the Base of the heart?
a: Origin point for all major blood vessels.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Fibrous Skeleton?
a: At the junction of atria and ventricles, surrounding the heart valves.

q: What is the physiological function of the Fibrous Skeleton?
a: Origin/insertion for cardiac muscle; pulls apex and base together during contraction; acts as an electrical insulator blocking direct transmission from atria to ventricles.

q: From where does the Superior Vena Cava receive blood, and where does it send it?
a: Receives from systemic veins (upper body); Sends to Right Atrium.

q: From where does the Inferior Vena Cava receive blood, and where does it send it?
a: Receives from systemic veins (lower body); Sends to Right Atrium.

q: From where does the Pulmonary Trunk (Artery) receive blood, and where does it send it?
a: Receives from Right Ventricle; Sends to Lungs.

q: From where do the Pulmonary Veins receive blood, and where do they send it?
a: Receives from veins of the lungs; Sends to Left Atrium.

q: From where does the Aorta receive blood, and where does it send it?
a: Receives from Left Ventricle; Sends to Systemic Arteries.

q: What is the primary physiological function of heart valves?
a: Ensure one-way flow of blood through the heart.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Tricuspid Valve?
a: Between the right atrium and right ventricle.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Bicuspid (Mitral) Valve?
a: Between the left atrium and left ventricle.

q: What is the anatomical location of Chordae Tendineae?
a: Fasten to the edges of AV valve flaps on the ventricular side and connect to papillary muscles.

q: What is the physiological function of Chordae Tendineae?
a: Prevent AV valves from prolapsing (being pushed back into the atrium) during ventricular contraction.

q: What is the anatomical location of Papillary Muscles?
a: Walls of the ventricles.

q: What is the physiological function of Papillary Muscles?
a: Provide stability for the chordae tendineae (they do not actively open/close valves).

q: What is the anatomical location of the Aortic Valve?
a: Between the left ventricle and the aorta.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Pulmonary Valve?
a: Between the right ventricle and the pulmonary trunk.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Right Coronary Artery (RCA)?
a: Originates at the root of the aorta, runs in the coronary sulcus on the right side.

q: What is the physiological function of the Right Coronary Artery (RCA)?
a: Feeds the right atrium, most of the right ventricle, some of the left ventricle, and the posterior interventricular septum.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Left Coronary Artery (LCA)?
a: Leaves the left side of the aorta.

q: What is the physiological function of the Left Coronary Artery (LCA)?
a: Branches into the circumflex and anterior interventricular branches to feed the left side of the heart.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Circumflex Branch?
a: Runs around the left side of the heart to the posterior surface.

q: What is the anatomical location and function of the Anterior Interventricular Branch (LAD)?
a: Runs in a groove toward the apex. Feeds the left atrium, most of the left ventricle and interventricular septum, and some of the right ventricle.

q: Where do Coronary Veins empty their blood?
a: About 85% of venous blood empties into the Coronary Sinus (posterior heart), which drains directly into the right atrium.

q: Define Myocardial Infarction (MI).
a: Heart attack. Tissue damage caused by ischemia (lack of blood flow/oxygen) due to narrowed or blocked coronary arteries.

q: What percentage of myocardial cells are Autorhythmic Cells (Pacemakers)?
a: About 1%.

q: Do Autorhythmic Cells have organized sarcomeres or contribute to contractile force?
a: No, they do not have organized sarcomeres and do not contribute to contractile force.

q: What percentage of myocardial cells are Contractile Cells?
Why do Contractile Cells have a smaller sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) compared to skeletal muscle?
a: Because they rely partially on extracellular Ca2+.

q: What is the physiological function of Desmosomes in intercalated disks?
a: Strong connections that tie adjacent cells together, transferring force.

q: What is the physiological function of Gap Junctions in intercalated disks?
a: Electrically connect cells, allowing waves of depolarization to spread rapidly, ensuring simultaneous contraction.

q: What is Step 1 of Cardiac EC Coupling?
a: Action potential enters a contractile cell from an adjacent cell via gap junctions.

q: What is Step 2 of Cardiac EC Coupling?
a: Action potential moves across the sarcolemma and into the t-tubules, opening voltage-gated L-type Ca2+ channels.

q: What is Step 3 of Cardiac EC Coupling?
a: Ca2+ enters the cell from the extracellular fluid down its electrochemical gradient.

q: What is Step 4 of Cardiac EC Coupling?
a: Ca2+ entry opens ryanodine receptor (RyR) Ca2+ release channels in the sarcoplasmic reticulum (Ca2+-induced Ca2+ release).

q: What is Step 5 of Cardiac EC Coupling?
a: Stored Ca2+ flows out of the SR into the cytosol, creating a localized Ca2+ spark.

q: What is Step 6 of Cardiac EC Coupling?
a: Multiple sparks summate to create a unified Ca2+ signal (90% of Ca2+ comes from SR, 10% from ECF).

q: What is Step 7 of Cardiac EC Coupling?
a: Ca2+ ions bind to troponin, initiating the cycle of crossbridge formation (myosin binds to actin) and filament sliding (contraction).

q: What is Step 8 of Cardiac EC Coupling (Relaxation)?
a: Relaxation occurs as cytoplasmic Ca2+ concentrations decrease, causing Ca2+ to unbind from troponin. Myosin releases actin.

q: What is Step 9 of Cardiac EC Coupling (Relaxation)?
a: Ca2+ is pumped back into the SR via Ca2+-ATPase.

q: What is Step 10 of Cardiac EC Coupling (Relaxation)?
a: Extracellular Ca2+ is removed from the cell via the Na+-Ca2+ exchanger (NCX) antiporter.

q: What is Phase 4 of the Action Potential of Myocardial Contractile Cells?
a: Resting membrane potential: Stable at about -90 mV (equilibrium potential for K+).

q: What is Phase 0 of the Action Potential of Myocardial Contractile Cells?
a: Depolarization: Gap junction depolarization triggers voltage-gated double-gated Na+ channels to open. Rapid Na+ influx depolarizes the cell to about +20 mV.

q: What is Phase 1 of the Action Potential of Myocardial Contractile Cells?
a: Initial repolarization: Na+ channels close. Cell begins repolarizing as fast K+ channels open, allowing K+ to leave.

q: What is Phase 2 of the Action Potential of Myocardial Contractile Cells?
a: The plateau: Action potential flattens out due to decreased K+ permeability and increased Ca2+ permeability.

q: What is Phase 3 of the Action Potential of Myocardial Contractile Cells?
a: Rapid repolarization: Plateau ends as Ca2+ channels close and slow K+ channels open. Rapid K+ efflux returns the cell to resting potential.

q: Why is the refractory period in myocardial contractile cells so long?
a: It lasts almost as long as the entire muscle twitch to prevent summation and fatal tetanus.

q: Define Pacemaker Potential.
a: The unstable membrane potential of autorhythmic cells that slowly drifts upward from -60 mV toward threshold.

q: What happens in Step 1 of the Autorhythmic Action Potential?
a: At -60 mV, If channels (funny current channels) open. They are permeable to both K+ and Na+.

q: What creates the steep rising/depolarization phase in Autorhythmic Cells (Step 4)?
a: Voltage-gated Ca2+ channels open and Ca2+ rushes in (unlike neurons/muscle where this is driven by Na+).

q: What is the anatomical location of the Sinoatrial (SA) Node?
a: Located in the right atrium.

q: What is the physiological function of the Sinoatrial (SA) Node?
a: Serves as the main pacemaker setting the heart rate.

q: What is the anatomical location of the Atrioventricular (AV) Node?
a: Located near the floor of the right atrium.

q: What is the physiological function of the Atrioventricular (AV) Node?
a: Functions to route electrical signals and slow them down (AV node delay).

q: What is the physiological function of Purkinje Fibers?
a: Transmit impulses very rapidly (up to 4 m/sec) outward among contractile cells from the apex toward the base.

q: Why is the AV Node Delay essential?
a: It allows the atria to complete their contraction and completely fill the ventricles before ventricular contraction begins.

q: Define Ectopic Pacemaker.
a: An out-of-place pacemaker. Parts of the conducting system take over pacing if the SA node fails.

q: Define Complete Heart Block.
a: Conduction from atria to ventricles through the AV node is completely disrupted. Atria and ventricles fire at their own disconnected rates.

q: Define Fibrillation.
a: Disorganized, uncoordinated myocardial contractions.

q: What does the P wave represent on an ECG?
a: Atrial depolarization.

q: What does the QRS complex represent on an ECG?
a: The progressive wave of ventricular depolarization.

q: What does the T wave represent on an ECG?
a: The repolarization of the ventricles.

q: Define Diastole.
a: The time during which cardiac muscle relaxes.

q: Define Systole.
a: The time during which cardiac muscle contracts.

q: What causes the First Heart Sound (S1)?
a: Closure of AV valves during isovolumic ventricular contraction (Lub).

q: What causes the Second Heart Sound (S2)?
a: Closure of Semilunar valves during isovolumic ventricular relaxation (Dup).

q: Define Stroke Volume (SV) and its equation.
a: The amount of blood pumped by one ventricle during one contraction. Equation: SV = EDV - ESV.

q: Define Cardiac Output (CO) and its equation.
a: The volume of blood pumped by one ventricle in a given period of time. Equation: CO = Heart Rate x Stroke Volume.

q: What is the Length-Tension Relationship (Frank-Starling Law of the Heart)?
a: As stretch of the ventricular wall increases (due to increased EDV), the stroke volume increases. The heart pumps all the blood that returns to it.

q: Define Preload.
a: The degree of myocardial stretch before contraction begins, representing the load placed on cardiac muscles.

q: Define Venous Return.
a: The amount of blood entering the heart from the venous circulation. Primary determinant of EDV.

q: Define Contractility.
a: The intrinsic ability of a cardiac muscle fiber to contract at any given fiber length.

q: Define Afterload.
a: The combined load of blood in the ventricle (EDV) and the arterial resistance during ventricular contraction. The force the heart must overcome to eject blood.

q: What are Cardiac Glycosides (Digitalis/Ouabain) and their mechanism?
a: Pharmacological positive inotropic agents that depress Na+-K+-ATPase activity, increasing cytosolic Ca2+ and thus increasing contractility.
